title: Excess mortality: Raw number of deaths from all causes compared to projection based on previous years
subtitle: The reported number of weekly or monthly deaths in 2020–2024 and the projected number of deaths for 2020, which is based on the reported deaths in 2015–2019.
caption:
Excess mortality: Raw number of deaths from all causes compared to projection based on previous years, United States
The reported number of weekly or monthly deaths in 2020–2024 and the projected number of deaths for 2020, which is based on the reported deaths in 2015–2019.

Weekly death counts in the United States from early 2020 through early 2021 are plotted against a projected baseline derived from 2015–2019 data, with the vertical axis ranging from 0 to 100,000 deaths. The orange line representing 2020 shows the highest values among the visible years, rising from approximately 60,000 in January to a peak near 80,000 in late April, falling to roughly 58,000 in June, and climbing again to exceed 85,000 by January 2021. The purple line for 2021 begins near 88,000 in early January, drops to the 60,000 range by March, and fluctuates between 60,000 and 75,000 through the remainder of the year. The teal line for 2022 starts around 85,000, declines to the 55,000–60,000 band by spring, and remains in that lower range. The blue line for 2023 stays mostly between 55,000 and 65,000, with a rise toward 70,000 late in the year. The black line labeled "Projected, 2020" tracks a steady path near 60,000, while the gray lines for 2015 through 2019 cluster tightly below 60,000 for the entire period. The 2020 and 2021 lines sit well above the projected and historical baselines during multiple intervals, particularly in the first quarter of 2020 and the final quarter of 2020. The visible pattern shows 2020 reaching the highest weekly death counts, with 2021 starting at a comparable level before declining, while 2022 and 2023 remain closer to the projected baseline for much of the year.
